summarization.a: This subfigure shows the formation of a triply bonded (TB) TMPS configuration on amorphous silica. Three surface silanol groups react with TMPS, releasing three methanol molecules and forming a TB SMI with Si−O linkages. The right step shows conceptual removal of the SMI (−SiPr) and hydrogen saturation (+3 H•) to generate the "TB residue" structure for energy calculations.
summarization.b: This subfigure illustrates how the TB bonding penalty (ΔE_TB-pen) is calculated: the energy difference between the "Relaxed Model" (pristine optimized surface) and the "TB Residue" (surface geometry after TB SMI removal). This quantifies the strain introduced into the silica framework by TB bonding.

a.question: What does the ΔE_TB-pen value quantify in this computational scheme?
a.answer: The TB bonding penalty quantifies the energetic cost of deforming the silica surface when three silanols must reposition to bond to a single TMPS molecule. By comparing the TB residue energy to the relaxed surface, researchers isolate how much of the total reaction energy arises from surface strain. This is critical for predicting which TB configurations are thermodynamically accessible under ALD conditions.

a.question: How do ΔE_TB-pen values vary among different TB configurations on the A.1 model?
a.answer: A.1-O:123 has a penalty of only 45 kJ mol⁻¹, while A.1-O:146 shows 162 kJ mol⁻¹ and A.1-O:245 exhibits 459 kJ mol⁻¹, indicating TB formation is highly site-specific.

a.question: What do the dashed lines between OH groups in the "Relaxed Model" represent?
a.answer: The dashed lines represent hydrogen bonds between adjacent surface silanol groups, which form a network that stabilizes the silica surface structure.

a.question: Does TB TMPS formation improve blocking performance in AS-ALD?
a.answer: Yes. The TB configuration consumes all three methoxy groups, maximizing chemical passivation and reducing potential nucleation sites for Al₂O₃ growth.
